frameworks.name: feature-specific channels for visual working memory
frameworks.one_line: early feature-selective channels maintain simple visual features and are vulnerable to same-dimension distractors

frameworks.name: awareness-gated interference
frameworks.one_line: conscious access is required for a distractor to bias a held orientation memory

frameworks.name: signal-detection theory
frameworks.one_line: memory reliability quantified via noise and systematic bias in orientation reports

core_question: Does a task-irrelevant orientation distractor bias visual short-term memory for orientation, and does this depend on awareness and task relevance?
key_assumptions: visual short-term memory for simple features is supported by feature-specific channels at an early processing stage
memory precision and bias can be read out from orientation report distributions
dichoptic suppression can render a stimulus unconscious while preserving sensory input
manipulations: presence and orientation difference of a mid-delay distractor grating
task-relevance of the distractor (to-be-ignored vs second memory target)
awareness of the distractor (visible vs dichoptically suppressed)
key_findings: memory for the initially presented orientation became noisier when it differed from the distractor
responses were shifted toward the distractor orientation by roughly 3 degrees
when the distractor became a second memory target, the attraction bias of the first report toward it was much reduced although reliability still dropped with orientation difference
biases caused by the distractor disappeared when it was rendered invisible via dichoptic suppression
interpretations: feature-specific channels supporting visual short-term memory are susceptible to interference from same-dimension inputs
the systematic bias from a distractor requires conscious perception of that distractor
limitations_noted: title metadata in source mismatches abstract content
